title: Chapter 8: Architecture of Despair
paragraphs: Agdash amazed and oppressed at the same time. The city was built on the multicentury remains of a fallen civilization, and its architecture screamed of the madness of a passing era. Graceful, upward-curved roofs in the style of old oriental pagodas, decorated with tarnished gold, curiously adjoined massive, ancient arches adorned with intricate runic patterns. And this entire fragile framework rested on heavy, oppressive Gothic buttresses of black basalt. It was a city-chimera, ugly and beautiful at once, fashioned from the remnants of different elven cultures who desperately clung to life, hiding behind common walls.

But behind the grotesque grandeur of the facades, a progressive rot was hidden. When Avotu and Alexander wove through the dense, tormented crowds through the narrow cobbled streets, they felt the thick, suffocating tension. Agdash was boiling. In the air, saturated with soot and cheap incense, was the надрывный cry of heralds in crimson capes. They monotonously, with mechanical voices, read out endless lists of general mobilization. Wives, sobbing, clung to the cloaks of departing husbands. Elven teenagers, barely holding ill-fitted spears in thin hands, dully marched toward the Gates. In the alleys, spontaneous protests broke out: exhausted, hungry, and ragged residents demanded bread and an end to the senseless massacre that was cutting down their people at the root.

"This world isn't just dying from an external threat," Alexander thought with the heavy clarity of an analyst, carefully studying the gray, sunken faces of the protesters. "It is devouring itself from within. Its resource is exhausted. By the laws of sociopathics, the fall of this society is a matter of a few months." For him, a man used to the cruel but structured logic of modern earthly megacities, Agdash looked like a colossus with feet of clay.

The royal palace rose in the very center of this abscess, surrounded by a wide and hot ring of lava moats. The King's palace guard, recognizing Avotu by his sinister black flame, parted in sacred terror, letting them in without a single question.

Inside the colossal throne room, a piercing, almost tangible gloom reigned. Instead of the expected pomp and gold, it smelled of old, moth-eaten parchment, melting wax, and multicentury, unbearable fatigue.

On a throne carved from a single piece of polished obsidian sat King Agsham. Of the former majestic ruler of fire elves, whose legends scared neighboring continents, not a trace remained. In front of Avotu sat a deep, emaciated old man. His famous 'living flame' on his head had turned into rare, smoldering crimson coals, threatening to go out forever any moment. The skin had thinned like parchment, tightly stretched over the skull, and the long fingers, convulsively clutching the armrests of the throne, more reminded of a vulture's claws clutching carrion.

"Avotu... Sivarus's Last Disciple," the king rasped. His voice sounded like the slow grinding of a tombstone against stone. Behind him, motionless as statues, stood only two silent guards in hollow, impenetrable helmets. "You've returned. I'd say I'm glad for the sake of politeness, but at my age, lies are too physically difficult."

"I've come for answers, your majesty," Avotu said. The fire elf bowed his head low, but there was no submission or fear in his gesture. Only deep, bitterness-soaked respect for how cruelly time had spared this old man. Alexander remained standing a tactical, wordless ghost half a step behind the hero.
The king smiled bitterly, joylessly, and this dry sound turned into a heavy, wet, lung-tearing cough, from which the old man bent in half.

"Answers? Still searching for the trail of Vulgar? Your lost family? Conspiracies..." the old man waved an иссохшей hand vaguely, with infinite tiredness toward the heavy stained-glass windows. "My boy. Your precious brothers aren't creating portals. They didn't turn against us. They are dead. Rotted. Like we all are."

The king's words sounded in the huge, empty hall like the click of a guillotine. Avotu staggered as if from a physical, powerful blow with a hammer in the chest. The black flame on his head painfully, convulsively contracted.
"But Zakhan's demons... they're trying to find something..." the great warrior's voice broke.

"Zakhan's demons are clinging to the sweet, intoxicating illusion of control, like you," Agsham interrupted him. He leaned forward in the chair with difficulty, and in his dull eyes, for a second, flared a dangerous, angry reflection of the former holocaust. "Reality is torn, Avotu. Portals aren't someone's evil will that can be killed with a sword. It's not a conspiracy of secret robes. It is absolute, terminal gangrene. The very fabric of the world is rotting and disintegrating into basic atoms. We are not a mathematical error, we are a corpse. The basis of physics is destroyed. We cannot stop this. Zakhan and his pitiful hordes, the Vampire Lords with their obedient legions of the dead... we are all locked in the same sinking cage, desperately and absolutely pointlessly trying to plug the holes in the ship's hull with the corpses of our citizens."

A thick, unbearably heavy silence fell. Alexander, watching everything from the side, looked at the king and saw the desperate, broken CEO of a bankrupt corporation who is simply stalling for time and numbers until the inevitable closing, feeding the assets into the abyss.

"I send children to the slaughter every day so that this cursed city exists for another insignificant month," Agsham said quietly, killed. He leaned back in the obsidian throne, closing his eyes. "There are riots in the city because my people understand the truth faster than I do. They are bleeding at the outposts. So if you came to look for ghosts of salvation, Avotu—leave. Leave this folly to the youngsters. If you came to die with remnants of honor—take your sword and stand on the wall. Nothing more remains in this rotting world."
